diagrams.title: String Memory Layout in C
diagrams.description: Shows how strings are stored in memory as character arrays with a null terminator, helping students understand why strings must be large enough and how indexing works.
elements: Character array diagram showing 'H', 'e', 'l', 'l', 'o', '\0' in consecutive memory cells
Label showing the null terminator '\0' as the string end marker
Memory address labels showing contiguous allocation
Annotation showing string length vs array size
diagrams.purpose: Explains the fundamental concept that strings in C are character arrays terminated by a null character, which is crucial for understanding all string manipulation functions.

diagrams.title: Common String Functions Classification
diagrams.description: A visual categorization of the main string manipulation functions in C: copying, concatenation, comparison, searching, and length, showing what each function does conceptually.
elements: Five category boxes: Copying, Concatenation, Comparison, Searching, Length
Function names in each box: strcpy/strncpy, strcat/strncat, strcmp/strncmp, strlen, strchr/strstr
Arrow or flow showing input to each function category
Color-coded boxes for easy visual differentiation
Brief icon or symbol representing each function type action
diagrams.purpose: Provides an organized overview of all string manipulation functions, helping students know what functions are available and when to use each one.

diagrams.title: strcpy and strcat Operation Visualization
diagrams.description: Shows before and after states when using strcpy to copy a string and strcat to concatenate strings, with visual representation of source and destination memory areas.
elements: Two separate memory layout sections for strcpy and strcat
Source string box showing original content
Destination string box showing before state (uninitialized or original)
Destination string box showing after state with copied/appended content
Arrow indicating direction of copying or concatenation
Null terminator shown in final positions
Step numbers (1, 2) showing the operation sequence
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates the practical effects of the two most commonly used string functions, helping students understand how memory is modified and the importance of proper buffer sizing.
